summary: Martin argues that 'Agile in the Large' is a contradiction in terms because Agile was specifically designed to solve small-project problems, not large ones. He traces software development from the 1960s through waterfall's necessity in the 70s-80s (due to long feedback loops) to Agile's emergence when Moore's law enabled sub-second turnarounds. The key insight is that humans have always known how to execute large projects through hierarchy and command-and-control—what we lacked was an effective approach for small teams. Agile solved the small problem; large projects are solved by breaking them into many small Agile teams coordinated through traditional management structures.
key_points: Agile was a 'turning of the wheel' back to 1960s-style short feedback cycles, but with added disciplines like testing, refactoring, and automation
Waterfall emerged from necessity—when edit/compile/test loops took 24 hours, extensive planning was required
Humans have always excelled at large projects (Apollo, D-Day, global economy)—the 'big' part was never the problem we needed to solve
Agile teams are the lowest level of command and control—'the squad'—above which traditional hierarchy becomes necessary
Agile in the Large approaches are really traditional project management with Agile vocabulary and flavor
money_quotes: Agile was a revolution in how relatively small teams can develop relatively small software projects. Note the emphasis on the word small.
We've always known how to do big projects. You do big projects by breaking them up into a bunch of small projects. Agile solved the small part of that.
Agile was never about egalitarianism. Agile was never about the rejection of plans, or the rejection of command and control.
Indeed, Agile was the embodiment of the lowest level unit of command and control: The squad.
Once you are doing something big, you have left the realm of Agile.
We went from 24 hour turnarounds in 1970, to 60 minute turnarounds in 1980, to ten minute turnarounds in 1990, to ten second turnarounds in 2000.
You don't design, build, produce and sell hundreds of millions of iPhones without an awful lot of command and control.
key_insight: Agile was designed to solve small-team, small-project problems—large projects require traditional command-and-control structures, and calling those structures 'Agile' is just adding flavor to millennia-old human organizational patterns.
